The Invitation

Peru calls you — not as a tourist, but as a traveler of the inner landscape.

This is an eight-day immersion in the Sacred Valley, where ancient mountains hold memory and the land itself is a teacher. You will wake surrounded by the Apus — the sacred mountains — and be held by the living intelligence of Pachamama.

We move slowly and intentionally.
Ceremony, fire, breath, and the silence of the Andes.

Through shamanic ritual, plant medicine, and somatic practices, you are invited to release old stories, open your heart, and encounter the unknown — which is always where transformation begins.

When we arrive in a place as potent as Peru, something in us softens. Awe does that. The Apus humble us, nourish us, and relax the nervous system into receptivity.

This is an intentionally intimate journey — only 8 participants. The small size is not incidental; it is the container. It allows for real presence, personal attention, and the kind of depth that is only possible in a small circle.

What You Will Experience

Eight Days of Deep Immersion

01

Meditate, breathe, move, and share — in nature and in circle

02

Somatic tools, breathwork, energy work, and active meditation

03

Ceremonial use of Wachuma (San Pedro cactus) — optional

04

Despacho offerings and sacred ritual with Andean shamans

05

Engage with the Quechua community and their cosmovision

06

Cleansing and transmission rituals at sacred power sites

07

Fire circles, music, song, and dance under Andean skies

08

Rest, integrate, and simply be held by the land

Day by Day

The Journey

We suggest arriving in the Sacred Valley a day or two before the retreat begins — to settle in gently, breathe the mountain air, and let the land receive you before the journey starts.

Day 1 · Nov 21

Arrival in the Sacred Valley

Welcome circle. Sharing intentions. Settling into the embrace of the Apus.

CircleIntentions

Day 2 · Nov 22

Viatcha Lakes + Pisac Ruins

Despacho ceremony at Viatcha lake. Afternoon hike to the Pisac ruins — ancient terraces above the valley.

DespachoPisac

Day 3 · Nov 23

Killarumiyoq — Temple of the Moon

Full day Wachuma ceremony with an Andean shaman at this extraordinary lunar temple. Evening integration.

WachumaCeremony

Day 4 · Nov 24

Ñaupa Huaca — The Portal

Morning meditation and group session. Afternoon: journey to Ñaupa Huaca — a pre-Inca sacred portal for deep revelation ritual.

PortalRitual

Day 5 · Nov 25

Amaru Weaving Community

Visit a living Quechua weaving community. Traditional dances, natural dyes, ancestral knowledge — and a homemade lunch.

CultureCommunity

Day 6 · Nov 26

Lares Hot Springs + Hike

A day in nature — hiking through the Andes to the thermal waters of Lares. Movement, mineral waters, and stillness.

NatureHot Springs

Day 7 · Nov 27

Urco + Water Circuit + Farewell Dinner

Morning Despacho. Sacred site visit at Urco. Hot water circuit and sauna. Farewell dinner at Pisac's finest restaurant.

DespachoUrcoSauna

Day 8 · Nov 28

Closing Ceremony

A heartfelt closing circle. Honoring the journey, integrating its gifts, and carrying the medicine forward.

IntegrationClosing

Optional extension: A Machu Picchu package can be added to your retreat on request. Details and pricing available upon inquiry.

The Land

Sacred Sites

Killarumiyoq

An Inca ceremonial site carved into a massive limestone boulder — a crescent moon in bas-relief, seven ascending steps, and the silhouette of a puma visible from a distance. Mama Quilla, the moon goddess, governed the lunar calendar, fertility, marriage, and the organization of ceremonial time. What makes this site distinct is the interplay of masculine and feminine energy through its cave structures. The feminine current here acts as a conduit — not in opposition to the masculine, but in balance with it — creating what practitioners describe as a portal for clarity, intention, and new beginnings. Local women still visit for fertility rituals, and the Killa Raymi festival continues each August. Almost no mainstream tourism reaches it.

Ñaupa Huaca

A pre-Inca site whose name translates to "Place of the Ancestors." Its central feature is a precisely carved altar in blue-grey andesite and a false door cut directly into the rock face. It sat on the Inca ceque system — the ritual pathways radiating from Cusco along which sacred sites were placed. The main carving aligns with the Southern Cross constellation. The stonework is qualitatively unlike standard Inca construction — smooth, geometrically exact, on one of the hardest stones available — suggesting it may predate the empire. Despacho ceremonies continue to be performed here today.

Pisac Ruins

Built under the emperor Pachacuti in the 15th century, Pisac served as military stronghold, agricultural center, and temple complex simultaneously. Its Intihuatana — a stone solar marker — was used to track solstices and fix the timing of planting and ceremony. One of the largest Inca cemeteries ever found is here: hundreds of tombs carved into the cliffs. The terraces below are still farmed. At the winter solstice, the sun hits the Intihuatana in a way that makes the astronomical purpose impossible to miss.

Urco

Built at the foot of Pitusiray Mountain and documented as the estate of Inca Urqo, son of the emperor Huiracocha, who chose this location to honor Apu Pitusiray — the sacred mountain at the center of several Andean origin myths. The site includes terraces, a tower, and a carved stone that channels water toward a figure of a puma or serpent. In the Andean worldview, Apus are not symbols — they are understood as living presences governing weather, health, and harvest. Offerings are still made here, and it sits at the geographic center of the valley with Pitusiray in full view.

Who Guides You

Your Facilitators

Roey Klein

Retreat Facilitator

I am a seeker of life's truth and meaning. My path began at 21, walking Israel's National Trail — crossing the country on foot from the Galilee mountains to the Red Sea. That journey planted something that never left me: a longing for direct experience over inherited belief.

I went on to live in a sustainable permaculture community in Europe, then moved to the United States to study and work within Rudolf Steiner's anthroposophic philosophy, living alongside children and adults with special needs. There, I developed a deep awareness of the spiritual forces shaping human life — and gained patience, compassion, and the lived discipline of community.

A journey through South America introduced me to ancient indigenous traditions and plant medicine, and what I experienced there changed the direction of my life. Moved by my own transformation, I founded a retreat center on the coast of Ecuador — where I supported many people in finding their purpose and walking their own path of change.

Today, I share my life and work with my partner, Kiya. Together — in love and in purpose — we travel and create sacred journeys across different countries, inviting others to live in harmony and freedom, and to remember who they really are.

Kiya

Retreat Facilitator

I am endlessly enthralled by the mystery of the energy that moves both our inner and outer universes.

I was born and raised in Brazil, surrounded by spiritual healers, shamanic rituals, and channeling practices that shaped my path from the beginning.

For over twenty years, I have worked as a healer and guide — weaving shamanism, tantra, and somatic and primal work into a path toward deep self-understanding. I use ritual, active meditation, and trauma-informed practice to help people meet what's been buried and return to wholeness. That same depth of experience shapes my work as a curator - of retreat centers, of intentional spaces, of containers where beauty and safety allow real transformation to unfold.

I have also guided women back to their ancestral heritage and sisterhood — reclaiming our wild nature, authenticity, and freedom together. Much of this work, and my own unraveling, was cultivated during a five-year nomadic journey across the Middle East, Europe, Bali, and the Americas — where I also served as a bridge between the Amazon, its sacred plant medicines, and the indigenous communities who carry them.

I lived in the Sacred Valley of the Andes in Peru for four years, in deep connection with the Apus — the sacred mountains and ancient wisdom of sacred sites. I still return every year to guide pilgrimages and deepen that connection. It remains one of my spiritual homes.

I am stepping once again into the unknown — this time alongside my partner, Roey Klein. Together, in love and in purpose, we build sacred journeys across the world, offering others the same descent into mystery that continues to transform us.
